section.title: His Steadfast Love Endures Forever
section.sectionRationale: The climactic psalm of the Egyptian Hallel sung at Passover, this song of thanksgiving contains the most explicitly messianic verses of the whole collection, the rejected stone become the cornerstone and the cry of Hosanna, both quoted directly of Jesus at His triumphal entry.
section.thesis: The LORD whose steadfast love endures forever delivers His afflicted one from every surrounding enemy and death itself, exalting the very stone the builders rejected to become the cornerstone, the day of the LORD's own marvelous doing.
section.frameNote: Six movements: the opening call to give thanks, echoed by Israel, the priesthood, and all who fear the LORD, verses 1-4; refuge in the LORD proven better than trust in man, verses 5-9; victory over surrounding nations in the LORD's name, verses 10-14; life preserved to declare the LORD's deeds, verses 15-18; entering the gates of righteousness, verses 19-21; the rejected stone become the cornerstone and the cry of Hosanna, verses 22-27; and the closing return to thanksgiving, verses 28-29.

units.label: His loving devotion endures forever
units.note: the call to give thanks, echoed three times

verses.text: Give thanks to the LORD, for He is good; His loving devotion endures forever.
Let Israel say, "His loving devotion endures forever."
Let the house of Aaron say, "His loving devotion endures forever."
Let those who fear the LORD say, "His loving devotion endures forever."

units.label: Better to take refuge in the LORD
units.note: than to trust in man or princes

verses.text: In my distress I called to the LORD, and He answered and set me free.
The LORD is on my side; I will not be afraid. What can man do to me?
The LORD is on my side; He is my helper. Therefore I will look in triumph on those who hate me.
It is better to take refuge in the LORD than to trust in man.
It is better to take refuge in the LORD than to trust in princes.

units.label: In the name of the LORD I cut them off

verses.text: All the nations surrounded me, but in the name of the LORD I cut them off.
They surrounded me on every side, but in the name of the LORD I cut them off.
They swarmed around me like bees, but they were extinguished like burning thorns; in the name of the LORD I cut them off.
I was pushed so hard I was falling, but the LORD helped me.
The LORD is my strength and my song, and He has become my salvation.

units.label: I will not die, but I will live
units.note: life preserved to declare God's deeds

verses.text: Shouts of joy and salvation resound in the tents of the righteous: "The right hand of the LORD performs with valor!
The right hand of the LORD is exalted! The right hand of the LORD performs with valor!"
I will not die, but I will live and proclaim what the LORD has done.
The LORD disciplined me severely, but He has not given me over to death.

units.label: Open to me the gates of righteousness

verses.text: Open to me the gates of righteousness, that I may enter and give thanks to the LORD.
This is the gate of the LORD; the righteous shall enter through it.
I will give You thanks, for You have answered me, and You have become my salvation.

units.label: The stone the builders rejected
units.note: the cornerstone, and Hosanna, cited of Christ

verses.text: The {{even_pinnah|stone}} the builders rejected has become the cornerstone.
This is from the LORD, and it is marvelous in our eyes.
This is the day that the LORD has made; we will rejoice and be glad in it.
O LORD, save us, we pray. We beseech You, O LORD, cause us to prosper!
Blessed is he who comes in the name of the LORD. From the house of the LORD we bless you.
The LORD is God; He has made His light to shine upon us. Bind the festal sacrifice with cords to the horns of the altar.

units.label: You are my God, and I will give You thanks

verses.text: You are my God, and I will give You thanks. You are my God, and I will exalt You.
Give thanks to the LORD, for He is good; His loving devotion endures forever.

even_pinnah.gloss: The cornerstone, literally the head of the corner, the foundational stone that aligns and bears the weight of an entire structure. What builders discarded as unfit becomes, by God's own doing, the very stone the whole building depends on, quoted directly of Jesus in Matthew 21:42, Acts 4:11, and 1 Peter 2:7.

basic.context: This climactic psalm of the Passover Hallel moves a rescued worshiper, likely the king, from a cry in distress, through victory over surrounding enemies who swarmed like bees, to entering the temple gates in thanksgiving, and finally to the psalm's two most explosive lines, a rejected stone become the cornerstone and a cry of Hosanna for the one who comes in the LORD's name.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm's refrain, His loving devotion endures forever, framing the whole song, verses 1-4 and 29, teaches that every deliverance recounted here, from surrounding nations, from near death, from rejection, flows from God's unchanging covenant love, not from the worshiper's own merit.
basic.originalLanguages: The stone the builders rejected becomes the even pinnah, literally rosh pinnah, the head of the corner, verse 22, the foundational stone bearing the whole building's weight. Verse 25's cry, O LORD, save us, we pray, is the Hebrew hoshia na, which becomes the Greek Hosanna shouted at Jesus' entry into Jerusalem, Matthew 21:9.
basic.typology: The rejected stone that becomes the cornerstone, verse 22, is quoted by Jesus of Himself after the parable of the vineyard, Matthew 21:42, by Peter before the Sanhedrin, Acts 4:11, and by Peter again describing all believers as living stones built on Him, 1 Peter 2:4-7; and the blessing on him who comes in the name of the LORD, verse 26, is shouted by the crowds at the Triumphal Entry, Matthew 21:9.
basic.christ: This is the climactic Hallel Psalm Jesus and His disciples almost certainly sang together at the Last Supper, Matthew 26:30, on the very night He would be rejected like the stone this psalm describes. Jesus applies verse 22 directly to Himself, the stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone, Matthew 21:42, and the crowds at His Triumphal Entry shout verse 26's Hosanna and blessing directly over Him, Matthew 21:9, so that His whole path, rejection, cornerstone exaltation, and public acclamation, is traced by this single ancient psalm.
basic.god: God is the one whose steadfast love never ends, who becomes strength, song, and salvation for the afflicted, who disciplines severely yet does not give over to death, and who Himself, marvelously, exalts what human builders discard, making the very day of that reversal a day to rejoice in.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 21:42 and Acts 4:11 apply the rejected stone directly to Jesus. Matthew 21:9 records the crowds shouting this psalm's Hosanna and blessing at the Triumphal Entry. 1 Corinthians 15:25 and Hebrews 13:6 echo this psalm's confidence in the LORD's help and eventual, total victory.
basic.oneStory: A worshiper rescued from surrounding enemies and near death enters the temple gates in thanksgiving to celebrate a rejected stone becoming the cornerstone, a psalm that traces, centuries in advance, the whole shape of Christ's path, rejected by builders, hailed with Hosanna, and exalted by God's own marvelous doing to become the very foundation on which His whole church now stands.

context.literary: A royal thanksgiving psalm moving from communal liturgical refrain, verses 1-4, through first-person testimony of deliverance, verses 5-18, to a processional entry into the temple gates, verses 19-27, closing with a return to the opening refrain, verses 1 and 29, forming a complete liturgical circle of praise.
context.historical: Likely sung at a royal or national thanksgiving, possibly connected to the rebuilding of the Second Temple, Ezra 6:16, or David's own restoration after conflict, and used liturgically as the climax of the Passover Hallel and during the Feast of Tabernacles' processions with palm branches, echoed in John 12:13.
context.canonical: As the final psalm of the Egyptian Hallel, 113-118, this psalm closes the collection Jesus and His disciples sang at the Last Supper, Matthew 26:30, and supplies two of the most frequently quoted messianic verses in the New Testament, the cornerstone and the Hosanna.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To lead a delivered worshiper, and through him the whole worshiping community, from thanksgiving for rescue from enemies into a climactic celebration of God's surprising reversal, exalting what was rejected, as grounds for enduring, communal praise of His unending covenant love.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific historical deliverance and temple entry; it prescribes the ongoing pattern of taking refuge in God rather than in human strength, and it stands as prophetic Scripture whose stone and Hosanna language the New Testament identifies as directly, literally fulfilled in Christ.

debates.question: Who is the original historical speaker of this psalm, the one surrounded by nations and later entering the temple gates?

views.label: The Davidic king, celebrating a specific military deliverance and temple entry in Israel's monarchic history
views.case: The psalm's royal, processional language, and its combination of personal deliverance with entry into the LORD's house, fits the pattern of a king's thanksgiving liturgy following victory, such as those associated with David's reign.

views.label: The restored postexilic community personified as a single voice, celebrating the rebuilding of the temple and reestablishment of worship
views.case: The psalm's placement within the Hallel and its echoes of a community's rejection and vindication fit a setting like the rebuilding under Ezra, when the returned exiles, themselves scorned and opposed by surrounding peoples, dedicated the rebuilt temple, Ezra 6:16.

debates.bearing: Whichever historical setting is in view, the New Testament treats the psalm's stone and Hosanna language as reaching beyond its original occasion to speak directly and specifically of the Messiah, a deeper divine intention behind the human author's original words.

study.originalLanguages: The stone the builders rejected becomes the even pinnah, rosh pinnah, the head of the corner, verse 22, the load-bearing foundation stone of the whole structure. And verse 25's cry, hoshia na, save us, we pray, becomes the Hosanna the crowds shout over Jesus at His Triumphal Entry, Matthew 21:9.

typology.type: The stone the builders rejected, become the cornerstone, verse 22
typology.fulfillment: Quoted by Jesus of Himself, Matthew 21:42, by Peter before the Sanhedrin, Acts 4:11, and applied to all believers as living stones built on Christ, 1 Peter 2:4-7.

typology.type: Blessed is he who comes in the name of the LORD, verse 26
typology.fulfillment: Shouted by the crowds over Jesus at His Triumphal Entry into Jerusalem, Matthew 21:9, Mark 11:9, Luke 19:38, John 12:13.

study.christ: This is the climactic Hallel psalm Jesus and His disciples very likely sang together immediately after the Last Supper, Matthew 26:30, before He walked out to be rejected exactly as this psalm foretells. Jesus applies verse 22 directly to Himself after the parable of the wicked tenants, the stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone, Matthew 21:42, a claim Peter repeats before the Sanhedrin, Acts 4:11, and expands to describe the whole church as living stones built upon Him, 1 Peter 2:4-7. The crowds at His Triumphal Entry shout this psalm's very words, Hosanna, and Blessed is He who comes in the name of the LORD, Matthew 21:9, so that Christ's entire path, rejection, exaltation, and acclamation, is traced centuries in advance by this single psalm.
study.god: God is the one whose steadfast love never ends, who becomes His people's strength, song, and salvation, who disciplines severely yet never abandons His own to death, and who marvelously reverses human judgment, exalting the very stone that builders threw away to become the foundation of the whole structure.

crossReferences.note: Jesus quotes verse 22 directly of Himself, the stone the builders rejected become the cornerstone.
The crowds shout this psalm's Hosanna and blessing over Jesus at His Triumphal Entry into Jerusalem.
Peter expands the cornerstone imagery to describe believers as living stones built upon Christ, the rejected yet chosen and precious cornerstone.

study.oneStory: A worshiper rescued from surrounding enemies and near death enters the temple gates in thanksgiving to celebrate God's marvelous reversal, a rejected stone become the cornerstone, a pattern that traces, centuries beforehand, the entire path of Christ, rejected by builders yet hailed with Hosanna and exalted by God to become the very foundation stone on which His whole church, Jew and Gentile alike, now stands forever.

quiz.q: What refrain opens and closes this psalm, framing the entire song?

options.text: Give thanks to the LORD, for He is good; His loving devotion endures forever.
options.note: This refrain in verses 1 and 29 grounds every deliverance in the psalm in God's unending covenant love.

options.text: The wicked will be utterly destroyed forever.
options.note: This is not the psalm's opening and closing refrain, which instead celebrates God's enduring loving devotion.

options.text: Israel will never again face any enemy.
options.note: The psalm actually describes present enemies surrounding the speaker, not the absence of any future enemy.

options.text: The temple will stand forever without being rebuilt.
options.note: The refrain concerns God's loving devotion, not a specific claim about the temple's permanence.

quiz.q: According to verses 8-9, what is better than trusting in man or princes?

options.text: Taking refuge in the LORD.
options.note: This double declaration reorients all trust away from human power and toward God alone.

options.text: Trusting in one's own wealth and resources.
options.note: The verses specifically contrast trusting man and princes with taking refuge in the LORD, not personal wealth.

options.text: Forming alliances with foreign nations.
options.note: The verses reject reliance on human power broadly, which would include foreign alliances, in favor of refuge in the LORD.

options.text: Relying on one's own military strength.
options.note: The comparison given is refuge in the LORD versus trust in man or princes, not specifically military self-reliance.

quiz.q: What does verse 22 declare about the stone the builders rejected?

options.text: It has become the cornerstone.
options.note: This verse is quoted by Jesus of Himself and by Peter of Christ throughout the New Testament.

options.text: It was thrown into the sea and lost forever.
options.note: The verse says the opposite; the rejected stone becomes the most important stone, the cornerstone.

options.text: It was reused only as a doorstep, a minor function.
options.note: The verse specifically names it the cornerstone, the foundational stone bearing the whole structure's weight, not a minor function.

options.text: It remained rejected and was never used again.
options.note: The verse's whole force is the reversal: the once-rejected stone is now exalted as the cornerstone.

quiz.q: What Hebrew phrase in verse 25 becomes the Greek word Hosanna shouted at Jesus' Triumphal Entry?

options.text: Hoshia na, meaning save, we pray, or save now.
options.note: This cry is quoted directly in Matthew 21:9 and other Gospel accounts of the Triumphal Entry.

options.text: Shalom aleichem, meaning peace be upon you.
options.note: That is a separate Hebrew greeting, not the phrase behind Hosanna, which comes from hoshia na in verse 25.

options.text: Baruch haba, without any connection to save us.
options.note: While related blessing language appears in verse 26, the specific term Hosanna derives from hoshia na, save us, in verse 25.

options.text: Adonai roi, meaning the Lord is my shepherd.
options.note: That phrase belongs to Psalm 23, not to this psalm's Hosanna cry in verse 25.

quiz.q: How does Peter apply the cornerstone image of verse 22 in 1 Peter 2:4-7?

options.text: He describes believers as living stones built upon Christ, the rejected yet chosen and precious cornerstone.
options.note: Peter extends the psalm's cornerstone imagery to describe the church's identity as founded on Christ.

options.text: He denies any connection between this verse and Jesus.
options.note: Peter's application is a direct, explicit connection between this verse and Christ as the cornerstone.

options.text: He applies it only to the literal stones of Herod's temple.
options.note: Peter's application is theological and christological, concerning believers built on Christ, not merely literal temple architecture.

options.text: He applies it exclusively to future events with no present church application.
options.note: Peter's application is directly to the present identity of believers as living stones now, not solely a future event.

questions: This psalm's refrain, His loving devotion endures forever, frames every deliverance in the song. What deliverance in your own life do you need to reframe this week as evidence of God's unending covenant love rather than mere luck or coincidence?

The psalmist declares it is better to take refuge in the LORD than to trust in man or princes. Where are you currently placing more confidence in human strength, status, or strategy than in God Himself?

This psalm's rejected stone becomes the cornerstone, exactly the pattern of Jesus' own rejection and exaltation. Where in your life does something that felt like rejection or failure need to be reframed in light of God's pattern of marvelous reversal?

The crowds shouted this psalm's Hosanna over Jesus at His Triumphal Entry, even as He rode toward the cross just days later. How does holding together both the joy of Hosanna and the reality of the coming cross shape your own worship this week?

Peter describes believers as living stones built on Christ the cornerstone. How does your identity as one of those living stones, part of something Christ Himself is building, change how you view your role in your local church community?

translationNotes.note: Cornerstone renders the Hebrew rosh pinnah, literally the head of the corner, the foundational stone that aligns and bears the weight of an entire structure; this verse is quoted directly of Jesus in Matthew 21:42, Acts 4:11, and 1 Peter 2:7.
Save us, we pray translates the Hebrew hoshia na, which becomes the Greek Hosanna shouted by the crowds at Jesus' Triumphal Entry into Jerusalem, Matthew 21:9, John 12:13.

Bind the festal sacrifice with cords, up to the horns of the altar may alternatively be rendered as an instruction to join the festal procession with branches in hand up to the altar, a phrase connected to the palm branches waved at Tabernacles and echoed at the Triumphal Entry, John 12:13.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from biblehub.com/bsb/psalms/118.htm because the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error in this session. Verified 29 verses in order against the section map bounds Psalm 118:1-29, grouped into seven stanzas given its length. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) specific to this psalm was confirmed this session; the Truth For Life results for this passage were a broad survey sermon covering Ezra through Malachi, not specific enough to this psalm to cite, so sermon status is none.
